title: Harp Chord System — JSON reference
source: HarpTrefoil.tex (byte-exact mirror of HarpChordSystem.tex) via trefoil/rebuild.py
_pedagogy_reference: See TREFOIL.md at repo root for the complete pedagogical model.
_vocabulary_model: The pool is the full 118-fraction vocabulary. 42 path fractions instantiate the six trefoil cycles (2nds/3rds/4ths × CW/CCW). 76 reserve fractions are coloristic voicings used for substitution and variety. pool = paths ∪ reserve.

how_to_use.purpose: This is the machine-readable version of a 2-page lever harp handout. Load this file at the start of any chat about the harp chord system so you do not need to re-learn the notation from prose each time.
what_this_contains: Page 1 HarpChords table: 14 finger patterns × 7 starting degrees → named chord (section: chords_by_pattern_and_degree)
Page 1 trefoil + cycle tables: 3 diatonic cycles (2nds, 3rds, 4ths), each with 7 edges labeled for both directions (section: cycles)
Page 2 Trefoil Paths: two-handed voicings that walk each cycle (section: paths)
Page 2 Reserve: two-handed sonorities indexed by LH figure (section: reserve)
Notation key, interval-from-tonic alphabet, and figure-parsing rules (section: conventions)
critical_distinctions: FIGURE vs PATTERN. A pattern is just an interval sequence (e.g. '33', '333', '434'). A figure is a pattern with a starting-degree prefix (e.g. '133' = start on degree 1, then intervals 3,3). In the HarpChords table, row labels are bare patterns. Everywhere else, strings like '133' or '5333' are full figures.

Two figures producing the same chord is normal. Pattern 233 and pattern 434 give identical pitch-class sets at every starting degree — 434 is just 233 with its middle note displaced up one octave. The handout lists both because they are different physical fingerings.

CW vs CCW. Each cycle is a closed loop of 7 diatonic chords that starts and ends at I. CW and CCW are the two directions of traversal. Every edge has a CW-label AND a CCW-label — these describe the mood of the same edge in each direction.
how_to_use.latex_for_notation_files: The `latex` field on each chord renders through the handout's \rn macro and is the string to use verbatim in ABC, LilyPond, MEI, or MusicXML chord annotations.

roman_numeral_case.note: Quality is implied by case alone; never write 'Im' or 'iim' — use 'i' and 'ii'.

figure_parsing.definition: A figure is a string of characters from the interval alphabet. The FIRST character is the starting scale degree; each SUBSEQUENT character is the interval (inclusive) from the previous finger to the next finger.
figure_parsing.span_formula: span = 1 + Σ(interval − 1) per interval; intervals are inclusive on both ends.

examples.parse: start degree 1; then interval 3, then interval 3
start degree 9 (i.e. scale-degree 2 one octave up); intervals 3, 3

lh_rh_figure_pair_notation.definition: When two figures appear together (e.g. '133 933' or '433 A43'), the LEFT figure is the Left Hand (LH) and the RIGHT figure is the Right Hand (RH).

conventions.nd_marker: A dash (—) in the HarpChords table means non-diatonic: that pattern starting on that degree produces a chord outside the key's diatonic set.

instrument.chord_structure: All 28-framework chords are continuous ascending stacks of thirds split between hands at a midpoint; LH plays bottom, RH plays top.

1.note: Same pitch-class set as pattern 233 at degree 1; different physical voicing (middle note up an octave).

paths.description: Paired LH/RH voicings along the three diatonic cycles. CW ascends, CCW descends. 42 entries (3 cycles × 14 rows). Together with the 76 reserve entries these form the 118-fraction pool.

reserve.description: Single-sonority two-handed fractions (LH over RH). Each has one mood name. 76 entries of coloristic voicings held in reserve for substitution. Together with the 42 path entries these form the 118-fraction pool.
